statement.markdown: Given a binary tree root with nodes containing data and pointers to left and right children, implement a function to remove all nodes with a given target value. If multiple nodes have the same value, all such nodes must be removed.
statement.outputFormat: Root of the modified binary tree
statement.notes: Time complexity: O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the tree.

hints.text: Use a recursive or iterative approach to traverse the tree, and update the root based on the removal logic

editorial.title: Removing Nodes from a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The correct approach involves utilizing a recursive function to traverse the tree, and removing nodes based on the given target value.
editorial.bruteForce: Naive approach would involve creating a new tree with all nodes except the target value, resulting in exponential time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a recursive function to traverse the tree, we can update the root and child pointers accordingly, resulting in O(N) time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: This solution traverses the tree recursively, keeping track of the previous node, and updating the root based on the removal logic.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This approach is correct as it covers all nodes in the tree, and removes nodes with the target value correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run can be found in the provided solutions.
commonMistakes: Not properly updating the child pointers during removal
Not using a recursive approach with a clear base case

starterCode.javascript:
function solve(root, target) {}
function TreeNode(x) {this.val = x;}

functionSignature.python: def removeNodes(self, root: TreeNode, target: int) -> TreeNode:

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def removeNodes(self, root: TreeNode, target: int) -> TreeNode:
        if not root: return None
        if root.val == target: root = None
        else:
            root.left = self.removeNodes(root.left, target)
            root.right = self.removeNodes(root.right, target)
        return root

class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, x):
        self.val = x
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

def main():
    root = TreeNode(5)
    root.left = TreeNode(2)
    root.right = TreeNode(3)
    root.left.left = TreeNode(1)
    root.left.right = TreeNode(4)
    root.right.right = TreeNode(6)
    s = Solution()
    newroot = s.removeNodes(root, 2)
    print(newroot.val)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    input = sys.stdin.readline
    def solve():
        return

    solve()

    exit()
